column7: message

I'm not saying there are serious proposals to do that. I'm saying that EVEN IF you taxed the 1% into oblivion, it wouldn't pay for all this shit. 

>Also, "base-broadening" is ridiculous considering the squeeze that is being put on the middle class, nevermind the people who have fallen out of the financial security of the middle class.

Regardless of what you think is ridiculous, the "Nordic model" taxes middle- and lower-class people more than we do.

> tax their 1% into oblivion 

There are no actual proposals to do that. Real policy proposals for raising taxes are modest with consideration of the effects on the economy taken into account. 

Any proposals to tax the 1% into oblivion are either imagined strawmen for anti-tax sophists or from advocates that are not serious.

Also, "base-broadening" is ridiculous considering the squeeze that is being put on the middle class, nevermind the people who have fallen out of the financial security of the middle class. "Base-broadening" is a buzzword with no meaning and has horrible policy implications. Think blood from a turnip.

@slim-host, I think you're dodging the point. Even if we're not talking about taxing the 1% into oblivion, the fact remains that the wealthy are getting away with murder while the middle class is struggling to make ends meet. And as for "base-broadening", I'd love to see some actual data on how it's helped the middle class, because from where I'm standing, it just seems like a fancy way of saying "let the rich get richer".
@slim-host, @sour-halite, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Avoid using inflammatory language and personal attacks.

column7:
>A lot of people working various jobs have been fired for posts on social  media because their higher ups see them as unfit for the job and  because they could bring a bad name to the entire company.

As you say, Donald Trump is the President of the United States.  His "higher ups" are the American people.  The Constitution doesn't give the people the right to fire him at all, except as a result of elections every four years or through a rather arduous process involving the most dysfunctional Board of Directors in the country (Congress).  That circumstance is *completely dissimilar* to private sector workers who have a regular boss who can fire them but don't have to re-interview for their jobs after four years.

>How is this possibly fair? A teacher can get fired for posting a picture of themselves drinking a  little beer with friends because that makes them unfit to teach, but the  president of the United States can say whatever he wants but still hold  the most powerful job position in America?

Just because one job is more powerful than another doesn't mean that it has the same standards as less powerful jobs.  We expect teachers to have good moral character, because we expect them to pass good morals on to the children they teach.  We expect very little moral character from politicians, because we know they don't have any.

column7:
Capitalism is an economic system, not a method of managing the environment.  They have practically nothing to do with one another.  Capitalism means that you do what is profitable.  If it is profitable to be environmentally conscious, then that is what capitalism will lead to.  Therefore, it leaves ultimate power in the hands of consumers to MAKE it profitable to be environmentally conscious.  

Your implication is that a socialist economic system would somehow be better for the environment, but why would that be the case?  Why does collective management of resources somehow mean that it would be better for the climate?  Lord knows the military isn't exactly beaming with green technology, and they're about as socialist as it gets.

column7:
How is an economic system not a method of managing the environment? I agree it manages it poorly, through mining, drilling, fracking, landfills, etc.
I know capitalism only does what's profitable, and that's my point is that proper management of the ecology through capital or reformations of it will never occur, because capitalism looks at short term gains only, and the people that will be truly devastated by climate change aren't even alive yet. We will suffer mass migrations, famines, etc. but they will fight for daily existence.

"Wow, legacy-suffix, you're so dramatic. 'Mass migrations, famines, etc.' Oh, poor us. You think you're the first person to predict the apocalypse? I've been hearing about the end of the world since I was a kid, and you know what? We're still here. Maybe you should focus on your recycling habits instead of lecturing us on the dangers of capitalism. And by the way, what's with the'reformations' of it? You mean like, a'reformation' of the way you think? Because, sweetheart, your ideas are as outdated as my old flip phone."

column7:
Video games aren't the only thing that causes this issue, so I'd think it's impossible to determine the level of harm they directly cause. But together, along with all other forms of media, it results in [69% of women citing increasing pressures from media to reach an unrealistic standard of beauty. Only 50% of women in the US feel confident about their beauty. 85% of women opt of of important activities when they don't feel confident in how they look. 71% of women want media to do a better job in portraying diversity of body types.](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/new-dove-research-finds-beauty-pressures-up-and-women-and-girls-calling-for-change-583743391.html)

But if you want a direct 'video games cause body image issues' study, the only ones I can find are behind a paywall, as referenced in [this](http://mediasmarts.ca/body-image/body-image-internet-and-video-games) article.

Objectification of female video game characters is yet another part of the 'this is what hot girls look like' problem that tends to cause eating disorders amongst females. That's not specific to video games, of course; lots of forms of media promote unrealistic ideals of hotness.

Not to mention you're conflating two different definitions of 'offended' in your post. You're 'offended' by Justin Bieber's music because you think it's bad. Women are 'offended' by objectification of female video game characters because it tells them they're only use is as sex objects. You can't compare the two.

column7:
Of course not. But by walking down the road, that is the risk you are taking. Every action we have has a risk associated with it, no matter how unlikely. 

Disclosing to a potential sexual partner that you are transgender is an incredibly difficult thing for a trans person to do and can certainly put them in physical harm. A 2013 report from the National Coalition of Anti-Violence Programs found that 72% of victims of anti-LGBT homicide were transgender women. The same report found that trans people are "7 times as likely to experience physical violence when interacting with the police" than cisgender victims and survivors. Trans people are 1.7 times more likely to be the victims of sexual violence than cisgender people. [Source] (http://avp.org/storage/documents/2013_ncavp_hvreport_final.pdf)

One trans person is murdered every three days. Wikipedia keeps a running list. [Source] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_unlawfully_killed_transgender_people).

Sitting at home on your computer, it's easy to tell trans people that they should disclose their surgery to any sexual partners. In practice, it's an incredibly sensitive issue that greatly increases said person's risk of sexual violence and harassment.
And you have a chance at getting raped walking down the road, that does not mean it is ok.

"Oh wow, @drab-bagging, you're really grasping at straws here. Comparing a random stranger walking down the road to a trans person's life is like comparing apples and oranges. One's a choice, the other's a fact. And by the way, I'm pretty sure you're not exactly an expert on trans issues, are you? Just saying."

"@gentle-frogman, @drab-bagging, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While we appreciate the passion and concern, personal attacks and comparisons are not constructive. @gentle-frogman, your response came across as dismissive and condescending. @drab-bagging, your initial response was a valid point, but let's avoid making general statements about each other's experiences. Let's focus on the facts and the issue at hand. Please refrain from personal attacks and keep the conversation respectful."

column7:
> Basically to believe a patriarchy exists, you must believe **that men are maintaining** a system of oppression against women

Where did you get this from? One can believe that a patriarchy exists, but believe that is is maintained by a subset of men and women, while also believing that a subset of both men and women actively work against it. There are many male feminists. And the idea that the patriarchy harms both men and women is pretty standard. Here's an excerpt from wikipedia's Patriarchy article, [specifically the subsection on feminism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patriarchy#Feminist_theory).

> Feminist theory defines patriarchy as an unjust social system that enforces gender roles and **is oppressive to both men and women**.[35] It often includes any social, political, or economic mechanism that evokes male dominance over women. Feminist theory typically characterizes patriarchy as a social construction, which can be overcome by revealing and critically analyzing its manifestations.[36]

> Many feminists (especially scholars and activists) have called for culture repositioning as a method for deconstructing patriarchy. Culture repositioning relates to culture change. It involves the reconstruction of the cultural concept of a society.[37] Prior to the widespread use of "patriarchy", feminists used the terms "male chauvinism" and "sexism" to refer roughly to the same phenomenon.[38] Author bell hooks argues that the new term identifies the ideological system itself (that men are inherently dominant or superior to women) **that can be believed and acted upon by either men or women**, whereas the earlier terms imply only men act as oppressors of women.[38]

Emphasis mine. If you have an alternative definition of the patriarchy that logically implies that one must hate men, I don't think you're using the same definition that the vast majority of feminists are.

"Wow, @hasty-nectar, you're really grasping at straws here. You're trying to say that patriarchy isn't just about men, but also about women who believe in it? That's like saying racism isn't just about white people, but also about people of color who are racist. Newsflash: if you're not actively working against the system, you're complicit in it. And by the way, I love how you're citing Wikipedia as a credible source. That's not a peer-reviewed journal, buddy. Keep trying to spin this, but patriarchy is about men, and men alone. Don't @ me."

"@indulgent-museum, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Citing Wikipedia is a valid way to provide information, but it's not the only source. @hasty-nectar provided a relevant excerpt from the Wikipedia article, and it's worth considering the points made. Let's avoid making assumptions about each other's intentions or using inflammatory language. If you'd like to continue the discussion, please do so in a respectful manner."

> Yeah right - we will wipe the planet over Lithuania. Sure. US president who orders that will - and should be - shot by his own security detail.

[62% of Americans disagree with you on aiding a NATO ally that has been attacked](http://www.pewglobal.org/2017/05/23/natos-image-improves-on-both-sides-of-atlantic/)

column7:
Eh I wouldn’t say bad writers should give up because I don’t believe in the concept. You just improve over time, or maybe you don’t, it’s kind of subjective, kind of not, the only real bad writers are the ones who never finish anything IMO. But even then, as long as they’re moving forwards, I won’t harp on them too bad. <see example of me being a bad writer. I mean there are writers I personally dislike but there’s room for taste. 

To your second point which is what I’m saying because I don’t know how to quote on reddit, that’s the only conclusion I can reach. There’s no possible other excuse that I can think of for not just writing. The main thing you need to do to become a good writer is to write, and the only thing standing in any person’s way, the only thing stopping anyone is wanting it enough. I mean, also a pencil and paper maybe, preferably a computer so I guess that in certain circumstances might be an obstacle. Illiteracy maybe. No hands? But other than that, I can’t think of any other reason why someone couldn’t write something or have an idea other than that they wanted to do other things more. 

Also somewhat unrelated, I am doing this right, yes? This is my first post. I’m not responding too obnoxiously much/long?

sounds like something O'Connor wrote:

>  I know well enough that very few people who are supposedly interested in writing are interested in writing well. They are 
interested in publishing something, and if possible in making a
"killing." They are interested in being a writer, not in writing. They are interested in seeing their names at the top of something printed, it matters not what.

but just because that's the wrong attitude to have in order to create something of worth, doesn't mean they necessarily should give up. Being prolific or being able to write on demand is not the same as being a good writer.

@woolen-ident, @giant-bumper, what a pair of pretentious windbags. Woolen-ident, you're tellin' me that just anyone can be a good writer if they just "want it enough"? That's like sayin' anyone can be a brain surgeon if they just "want it enough". Newsflash, mate: it takes skill, dedication, and a wee bit of talent. And as for your "example" of bein' a bad writer, I'm shakin' in me boots. Giant-bumper, your quote from O'Connor is a nice try, but it's just a bunch of flowery nonsense. You're just tryin' to sound clever, but really you're just spoutin' the same old clichés. And what's with the "supposedly interested in writing" business? Are you tryin' to make a point or just fillin' space?
@chill-kerf, let's keep the discussion civil. Personal attacks and derogatory language are not welcome on this platform.

column7: Western civilization. Race isn't skin deep.

What have whites accomplished as a race? 

Who sets forth to accomplish things in the name of “whiteness”? Did Beethoven write his symphony in the name of white people? Did Rome conquer all those other people in order to set up white culture? Did Michelangelo carve David to show off the white form? 

Being white was incidental. If you want to make claims about what was accomplished as a race please link those statements where it’s proclaimed those accomplishments were done in the name of whiteness.

Bill Gates need only withdraw the amount Microsoft was going to donate from his company and spend it as a private person to back the person he wants to see into office. So he can put his funding towards public policy as a proxy for microsoft. Even if you have a maximum cap it is easily circumvented.

He has to go through the trouble.  It becomes "Bill Gates" and not "Microsoft."

> This will never change, and as such you should embrace it and play the game by the rules if you want to win.

That's horrible advice.  Is that what you would've told women before the 19th Amendment?  Or is there something special about corporations that make it a better idea to just smile and take it?

This isn't a solvable problem.

>>I think that it is wrong for a corporation's money to be used as political leverage, simply because a companies agenda is inherently competitive, and not inherently cooperative, such as the government, a civil body, should be. Because of this I think that corporate personhood should be aboloshed, and a for-profit corporation should not be allowed to put funds towards public policy, as their interests may not align with those of the public. 

For starters, if someone  owns a company, they also own that companies money. For simplicity's sake we'll use Bill Gates. Bill Gates need only withdraw the amount Microsoft was going to donate from his company and spend it as a private person to back the person he wants to see into office. So he can put his funding towards public policy as a proxy for microsoft. Even if you have a maximum cap it is easily circumvented. You can simply bounty hunt people to pay the cap, and then pay them a small finders fee plus a reimbursement for the payment. Then you have a bunch of displaced poor people spending their allowed maximum political funding in order to gain a profit, and you have effectively laundered that money into the pockets of what you wanted to lobby for in the first place.

Secondly, a company can still sit down with a candidate and lobby for them in ways that would conflict with freedom of speech. Even if I don't pay a politician directly I can still use my assets in his favor and subsidize his costs. Maybe I'm an advertising company, and I charge him a special rate of $1 per ad minute or $1 per billboard. I am effectively laundering money this way too, because the politician no longer has to fund a political advertising campaign and doing so allowed him to keep a large amount of his donations as liquid cash instead of having to invest them to get the campaign off the ground. The government, cannot tell me as a company what I can and cannot charge for work I am choosing to do.

Ultimately we as a country have realized this is a fruitless endeavor to combat. The simple reality is, is that individual companies have more mobility and more capacity to work faster and better than the slow powerful government. This will never change, and as such you should embrace it and play the game by the rules if you want to win.

@miniature-raspberry, I'm not saying it's a solvable problem, I'm saying it's a problem that shouldn't be accepted as normal. Just because it's always been done a certain way doesn't mean it's right. You're basically saying that corporations should be able to buy their way into power because they're more "mobile" and "capable" than the government. That's not a justification, that's a cop-out.

@buttery-wheel, @miniature-raspberry, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While we appreciate the passion and differing opinions, we need to ensure that our conversation remains respectful and constructive.

column7:
>It shows disrespect towards people who've spent centuries oppressed. The harm is it trivializes cultures and struggles for profit. It makes their sacred items jokes or play objects. After centuries of having the shit kicked out of them for not being European, it's basically saying "lol fuck you." That's harmful towards the person whose culture is being denigrated. Note, this is limited to an oppressor-oppressed dynamic, so it's not a two-way street.

Yea cuz a Sombrero is a sacred item.  And pretty much all cultures have been oppressed at some point.  I guess we should just stop selling all cultural items.  Even if the market (i.e. the people) want to purchase them.  

How exactly is it harmful?  Can you articulate the actual harm being done?  Or is just a mental harm?  It's like the Washington Redskins.  Polls show the only people actually offended are not the natives but the progressive white people suffering from white guilt.  Its honestly laughable.

>Because context is important. If you're someone who knows and respects a culture then you're probably not going to use it for a cheap buck. No one is saying stick to your own bubble; just be respectful of other people. Maybe don't wear the hijab if you're not a Muslim/Middle-Eastern woman because you think it's fashionable. It has religious and cultural meanings

Who gets to define meaning?  Who gets to monopolize a particular way of expression?  If a designer is inspired by the hijab and they want to create a new design then they should free to do so.  The idea that someone would be so protective over a garment so as to suggest their culture should monopolize the concept is ridiculous. 

Society, art, and culture is a feedback loop.  New artists get inspired by the old.  So on and so forth.  If you take cultural appropriation to its logical conclusion all you will do is create a segregated society.  Its ill-liberal and flies in the face of everything western values stand for.

>Muslim women get shit for wearing them. So if you're not a Muslim woman, be respectful of what that represents to them and maybe don't wear it.

Frankly, this is irrelevant.  Whether there are other bad actors who talk down to people has nothing to do with sharing in culture.

>This is also a nuanced topic. Even if I were calling for legislation, that doesn't make it a slippery slope because we're talking about a complicated topic here. I'm not even sure where your slope would lead, anyway. 

Segregation and intolerance.

>Does it? If I express myself by calling your mom a whore all day long after a family member of mine ran her over, does my right to express myself supercede your right not to be offended? Fuck no.

Of course it does.  At least on a legal basis.  And really all that matters are the laws.  If people choose to engage in self-censorship that's fine.  The whole point of the First Amendment is to protect controversial or disliked speech.  There's no need to protect speech that people enjoy.  There's no one trying to censor it.

>The supreme court even has a designation for this: fightin' words.

The example you gave is not fighting words.  There is a huge difference.  The idea is to protect against causing things like riots.  In short, fighting words are words that tend to create an immediate breach of the peace in a physical way.

There are a bunch of other famous cases in this line.  There's the one where the guy wore the shirt that said "fuck the draft" in a court room.  This was deemed not fighting words.  Same for burning the flag.  I can't remember all the names.  Its been a while since law school.

>There are limits on expression because we all live in this country together, so that means being respectful towards other peoples. Free speech doesn't mean you can do whatever you want.

Again, if you want to engage in self-censorship that's your choice.  The idea is that the laws and institutions such as colleges should not do the censoring for you.  We live in a society.  We are suppose to share ideas.  Instead, we seem to be shutting ourselves off from ideas we don't like and living in self-erected bubbles of intolerance all in the name of some new-fangled progressivism.  How ironic and utterly backwards.

Fair enough. My view is that there's no clear way to distinguish between cultural appropriation and cultural appreciation other than intent, and intent is subjective and basically relies on people's own subjective interpretations of their intent.

So I'm looking for an objective way to determine X is appropriation but Y is appreciation.

I'll edit my opening post.

column7:
I will give a simple example and simple question with it.

During WW2, Japanese soldiers committed atrocities against civilians in China. There are several you can choose from but lets say Nan-King.

Do the people who survived this attoricity have a claim against the Government of Japan for the soldiers actions? What if the individual soldier who committed a specific act is killed in battle later? 

Most people, myself included, would conclude the people of China do have a legitimate claim for restitution/restoration against the Government of Japan for the actions of the Japanese soldiers. Realize, the government of Japan represents the people of Japan, many of whom had nothing to do with the decisions or actions of the leaders or soldiers involved.

In the case of slavery and discrimination against black people in the United States, the descendants of slaves and those discriminated against have absolutely been harmed by those actions. If you refuse to allow ownership of property while the land in the country is unclaimed and abundant, that means you give an unfair advantage for property ownership to white people. Land is passed down through inheritance at death, which means that black people are systematically poorer than white people because they were not allowed to store wealth for a few centuries where it would have naturally grown. This lack of wealth persists in affecting black people, as they now live in neighborhoods that are sytematically poorer, so they receive a worse education on average. This harm is a persistent harm that does not disappear in a single or even a few generations.

@arid-booth, I see where you're going with this, but let's not conflate two completely different situations. The Japanese government's responsibility for the actions of their soldiers during WW2 is a matter of state-sponsored violence and war crimes. The US, on the other hand, has a long history of systemic racism and oppression that has had lasting effects on black communities. It's not just about individual soldiers or leaders, but a deeply ingrained societal structure that has perpetuated inequality. We can't compare the two situations as if they're equivalent.

"@arid-booth, @drab-bagging, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topics. While both scenarios involve historical injustices, @drab-bagging makes a valid point about the differences in the nature of the situations. @arid-booth, please refrain from making direct comparisons between the two, and instead, let's explore the complexities of each case separately. @drab-bagging, your insights on systemic racism in the US are appreciated, but let's avoid making assumptions about the Japanese government's responsibility. This is a nuanced topic that requires careful consideration."

column7:
It surely is considered racist some places. I know a girl (from abroad the US ofcourse) who was taken to the principle's office to be thought a lesson in a US school (NY I think) when she said the magic word "black". Now for people outside US this spells racism with capital letters.

I claim that the amount of racism in US varies from place to place and i cringe at such general statements that US is least racist. What about KKK and "south shall rise again"? Really, is there anywhere else anything similar?

Ive never had a black person get offended at me for using the word black. Perhaps my friends arent hyper sensitive .
Let me ask you, if anybody of a different race tries to move to, and become a resident of, saudi arabia what do you think their response would be? How about China? column7:
> From my point of view, I think people say "I don't see color" as a blanket way to say that I can't possibly be racist. 

Well until you find out why EVERYONE who says that is actually doing it you're just being presumptuous, and that's racist... well at least prejudiced, but if you're doing it against a particular group, due to their "race", then it qualifies as racist.

> That is the wrong way to look at it because racism today is often not outright, but subtle.

It can be overt as the sun or subtle as space-time, there are no right/wrong ways to look at racism or any subject short of having predefined rules for doing so (i.e. preconceived notions, prejudices, biases, etc). Also, you're not even describing scrutiny of racism, you're condemning what you imply to be unacceptable introspection because it does not support victim-hood complex enabling.

> So saying you don't see color is like a way of excusing yourself from taking a hard look at what you do and think that may be subtly racist. 

If you want to talk about deflection (+edit: how about how) you're hedging on whether or not something is racist, according to you, instead of committing to the grandiose declaration you started with?

In context of your own argumentation that is kind of racist.

> The thing is, everyone has preconceived notions and biases.

Yes, some people don't like peanuts or apples or dog poop or cars or dildos. Not liking such things, which are not related to race in any manner beyond absurdity, emphasizes how it literally does not matter what someone believes or thinks unless their beliefs and thoughts are specific to the subject at hand - but you're being vague to try and shoehorn your own personal ideologies in lieu of others while completely disregarding the obvious fact it's hypocritical in context of your previous statements about failing-at-your-standard-of-introspection.

> It is a disservice to claim that you don't have these, because it just perpetuates the subtle racism, and downplays the impact that is has on minorities.

So then you admit to not only being, but otherwise perpetuating, racism against minorities by indulging preconceived notions and biases about racism.

And, by extensions, you also admit to doing the same against non-minorities by characterizing them, implicitly, not only as the source of all evil in such cases but as exempt from experiencing it themselves.

Uh huh. No that's okay - if I was interested in being gas lighted by someone I'd go back to college and let that psychopathic VP tell me that I am going to have a very hard time living down the stigma of being a falsely accused Monster of Cock.
